headline: Lin’s answers show a clear pattern: late nights are tied to decision bottlenecks and default escalation, and she’s explicitly asking for scope reset and ownership boundaries.
summary_bullets: Lin links the last three weeks of late nights to “carrying decision bottlenecks and final reviews,” not to general workload.
She reports a feedback/decision gap: “I still do not get enough substantive feedback on key decisions,” which she connects to being “default escalation.”
She has a concrete delegation plan—“hand off one stream end-to-end and stay out of the middle”—but the conversation didn’t capture the specific stream, owner, or timeline.
understanding_paragraph: Lin is proud of the outcomes, but she’s drawing a hard line that the pace “should not be normal.” She’s not asking for more effort; she’s asking for a scope reset and clearer ownership so she stops being the default escalation point. The new thing is her explicit mechanism: decision bottlenecks plus final reviews are what’s driving the late nights.

axes.meaning: Running hot for weeks: late nights are tied to decision bottlenecks and final reviews, and she says the pace must not become normal.
Motivation is still outcome-driven, but the work feels heavier due to bottlenecks and her role as default escalation.
Strong signals of misaligned expectations: she lacks substantive feedback on key decisions and wants clearer ownership boundaries.
No clear growth trajectory signal; the only direction is operational (delegation boundaries), not learning or stretch.

brutal_truth_employee: Lin is explicit that the pace is not sustainable: “the pace should not be normal.” She also names the root cause and her role in it: “Late nights came from carrying decision bottlenecks and final reviews,” and she wants “clearer ownership boundaries so I am not default escalation.”
brutal_truth_manager: Lin gave a direct, actionable mechanism (“decision bottlenecks and final reviews”) and a concrete delegation intent (“hand off one stream end-to-end”), but the conversation didn’t lock down the specific stream, the new owner, or the decision points she’ll stop owning. Next time, turn her delegation plan into a specific scope/decision map with named ownership and an explicit handoff moment.

next_actions.action: Schedule a 30-minute scope reset with Lin and the relevant product leads to pick the one stream to hand off end-to-end and name the new owner.
Create a decision-boundary doc: list the key decisions, current bottleneck steps, and which decisions Lin will no longer escalate by default.
Confirm the handoff outcome by asking Lin: what moved to the team, what still requires her, and whether late-night drivers dropped after the reset.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Lin still says “default escalation” or late nights persist, treat it as the new normal and re-scope immediately.
Before next 1:1: if Lin cannot name the end-to-end stream owner and the exact decision points she’s stepping out of, the boundaries aren’t real yet.
